food_name: Potato Wedges with Tomato Sauce and Mayonnaise
aliases: Potato Wedges with Tomato Sauce and Mayonnaise

english: Potato Wedges with Tomato Sauce and Mayonnaise

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 covers potato wedge as a food product; the specific Italian preparation with tomato sauce and mayonnaise is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers potato products and dishes broadly; no specific code exists for Italian-style potato wedges with condiments. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for potato wedges as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of potato wedges with tomato sauce and mayonnaise is approximately 200 grams, comprising seasoned baked potato wedges accompanied by small portions of tomato dipping sauce and mayonnaise.

nutrient_source.title: Potatoes, baked, flesh and skin, without salt (USDA FoodData Central ID 170032) combined with condiment adjustment for tomato sauce and mayonnaise
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central entry for baked potato (flesh and skin, without salt, FDC ID 170032). Values adjusted upward for fat, sodium, and energy to account for the addition of mayonnaise and tomato sauce as served condiments, consistent with a mixed-dish estimation approach. The composite per-100g values reflect an approximate 80% potato wedge and 20% condiment (tomato sauce and mayonnaise) ratio by weight, which is a standard estimation for this dish type. Sodium elevated due to tomato sauce and mayonnaise contribution.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Baked potato (Russet, without fat)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for baked potato (mean GI ~72, high category). Potato wedges are a cut and baked form of potato; the GI is expected to be similar to baked potato. The addition of mayonnaise and tomato sauce may slightly lower the effective GI of the meal due to fat and acid content, but the per-100g GI is reported for the potato wedge base consistent with the analogous baked potato entry. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as: (72 × 41.0g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 29.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tomato sauce and mayonnaise
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful that the tomato sauce and mayonnaise condiments contribute approximately 210 mg sodium per 100g; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium condiments is advisable to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and significant carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Potato wedges have a high glycemic index of approximately 72, and a 200g serving delivers approximately 41g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~29.5; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with high-fibre or protein-rich foods to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose carefully after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat content from mayonnaise and cooking oil, including saturated and polyunsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mayonnaise component contributes to total fat intake (~5.8g fat per 100g) including saturated fat (~0.9g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and consider requesting the mayonnaise on the side or substituting with a lower-fat dipping option to reduce overall saturated fat and caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Patate a Spicchi is an Italian-style potato wedge dish consisting of thick-cut potato wedges that are seasoned with herbs and spices, then baked or roasted until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining fluffy and tender on the inside. As served at Italian restaurants in Singapore such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, the dish is accompanied by tomato dipping sauce and mayonnaise. The tomato sauce provides a tangy, slightly sweet counterpoint to the savoury wedges, while the mayonnaise adds richness and creaminess. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant food with approximately 145 kcal per 100g, 20.5g carbohydrate, 5.8g fat, and 2.4g protein. The high glycemic index (~72) means the dish can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (~210 mg per 100g) is moderate and primarily attributable to the condiments. Potatoes are a good source of potassium, vitamin C, and vitamin B6. This dish is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to the egg-based mayonnaise. It is typically served as a starter or side dish in Italian casual dining contexts.
common_user_queries: What are potato wedges with tomato sauce and mayonnaise?
How many calories in patate a spicchi?
What is the GI of potato wedges?

Nutritional information for potato wedges with dipping sauce

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Italian-style potato wedges with tomato sauce and mayonnaise was found in any primary approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central baked potato data (FDC ID 170032) with a composite adjustment for condiment contribution. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous baked potato entry (GI ~72). Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for baked potato and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the potato wedge base; the mayonnaise contribution to B12 is negligible and confirmed effectively zero per USDA data for the composite. Trans fat is 0.0 as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central for baked potato and standard commercial tomato sauce and mayonnaise formulations. Nutrient values represent an estimated composite of approximately 80% baked potato wedge and 20% condiments (tomato sauce and mayonnaise) by weight. The GI of 72 is based on the analogous baked potato entry from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of the composite dish may be slightly lower due to fat and acid from condiments. The food_group assignment of 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record as potatoes are classified as starchy staples in the Singapore HPB and FAO/INFOODS frameworks. Price data (SGD 15.81) is from field survey data and is not used for nutritional purposes. This dish is served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, Boat Quay, Singapore.
